title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about setting and completing fitness challenges. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Pushing the Limits: Four People on Fitness Challenges and Personal Goals

texts.B:
### Immaculata, 26, Junior Doctor
[45]I completed an open-water swimming challenge last year – crossing the English Channel as part of a relay team – which required nearly eight months of training in cold water.[/45] As a junior doctor working demanding shift patterns, finding time to train was genuinely difficult, but I refused to let the challenge become another thing I said I'd do one day. [49]What surprised me about cold-water swimming was how powerfully it affects your mental state – there is a clarity you feel in cold water that is hard to describe and impossible to ignore.[/49] The experience has made me curious about the therapeutic potential of cold-water exposure, which I'm now beginning to research in my spare time.
texts.D:
### Derek, 46, Sales Manager
[44]I signed up for a solo cycling challenge – cycling the full length of the UK from Land's End to John o'Groats – after a health scare that made me want to do something genuinely ambitious before it was too late.[/44] I had done very little cycling beforehand and the training schedule was brutal. [48]On day nine of the actual ride, I had a demoralising afternoon – headwinds, rain and a mechanical problem – and I sat on the roadside seriously questioning why on earth I was doing it.[/48] But I kept going, and reaching John o'Groats on day sixteen was one of the most emotional experiences of my life. [52]I've since completed two more long-distance cycling challenges, and my doctor now says my blood pressure is that of someone ten years younger.[/52]
texts.C:
### Chloe, 31, Primary School Teacher
I never thought of myself as a runner until my school organised a staff team for a local 10K charity run. [43]What began as a short fun run has turned into something I take very seriously – I've now completed three full marathons and I'm currently training for an ultra-marathon in Wales.[/43] The jump from marathon to ultra felt completely absurd when I first considered it, but my coach helped me believe it was achievable. [47]Running long distances has changed how I think about discomfort – I've learned that most of what feels like a physical limit is actually just a signal to slow down rather than to stop.[/47] [51]I started a running club at my school for the older pupils, and watching children discover they are more capable than they thought is more rewarding than any personal finish line I've crossed.[/51]
texts.A:
### Frank, 55, Landscape Gardener
[46]I've spent thirty years working outdoors, but I had never climbed a mountain until my son challenged me to join him on a charity trek to the Everest Base Camp.[/46] The altitude was something I was completely unprepared for – no amount of hill walking in the Peak District can prepare you for that. [50]Reaching base camp and looking out at the Khumbu Icefall was one of those moments where you feel very small and very fortunate at the same time, and I wept like a child.[/50] My son and I raised over twelve thousand pounds for a local hospice, and the experience has brought us much closer together. I'm now planning a trek in the Andes for next year.

questions.question: describes a progression from a modest starting point to an increasingly ambitious challenge?
questions.explanation: Chloe went from a short fun run to three full marathons and is now training for an ultra-marathon.

questions.question: was motivated to take on a serious physical challenge by a concern about their health?
questions.explanation: Derek signed up for the Land's End to John o'Groats challenge after a health scare that made him want to do something ambitious.

questions.question: completed a challenge in a natural environment that required a long period of specialist preparation?
questions.explanation: Immaculata crossed the English Channel as part of a relay team after nearly eight months of training in cold water.

questions.question: accepted a challenge that was proposed to them by a member of their family?
questions.explanation: Frank says he had never climbed a mountain until his son challenged him to join a charity trek to Everest Base Camp.

questions.question: describes developing a new way of understanding physical difficulty through their sport?
questions.explanation: Chloe says running long distances taught her that most of what feels like a limit is a signal to slow down rather than stop.

questions.question: experienced a serious low point during the challenge itself but managed to continue?
questions.explanation: On day nine, Derek sat on the roadside in the rain questioning why he was doing the ride, but kept going and finished.

questions.question: describes an unexpected effect on their mental state associated with their type of training?
questions.explanation: Immaculata was surprised by how powerfully cold-water swimming affects mental clarity in a way she describes as impossible to ignore.

questions.question: describes an emotional reaction upon reaching a significant point in their challenge?
questions.explanation: Frank says reaching Everest Base Camp was a moment where he felt very small and very fortunate and wept.

questions.question: mentions inspiring a group of young people through their own sporting activities?
questions.explanation: Chloe started a running club at her school and finds watching children discover their own capabilities more rewarding than her personal results.

questions.question: says their fitness challenge has produced measurable long-term benefits for their physical health?
questions.explanation: Derek says his doctor now reports that his blood pressure is that of someone ten years younger following his cycling challenges.
